conclusion: Part 1 traces Don Valentine and Sequoia from Fairchild/National sales mastery through Capital Group's 1972 "Sequoia Fund" ($5M) to independent Sequoia Capital (~$3–5M, three years raising). Don wrote modern VC: market-first thesis ("give me a giant market"), 20-word Socratic questions, active board participation, and ecosystem investing around semiconductor roadmaps. Portfolio drove ~$3.3T public market cap cited — but Apple early sale (~$6M gain vs hundreds of billions lost) and Atari/Warner exit teach "let winners run." Cisco (1987, $2.5M for 30%) became the refined playbook. Ben/David grade Don's first ~15 years an A (David argues A+ for inventing the industry).
background: Episode opens at Silicon Valley's 1957 birth: traitorous eight leave Shockley → Fairchild (Sherman Fairchild/Arthur Rock structure). Don (Yonkers, Fordham, Raytheon) joins Fairchild ~employee 40, scales sales ~$2M→$150M+, pioneers selling chips into new verticals despite east-coast parent board vetoing ecosystem investments — so Don angel-invests personally. 1972: Capital Group (American Funds, >$1T AUM lineage) backs Don's full-time startup investing. 1974: independent Sequoia Capital Fund I after brutal LP raise. Early bets: Atari (~$600K), Apple (1977, Mike Markkula via Don), EA, Oracle, 3Com, Cisco. Don recruits journalist Mike Moritz (1980s) and sales-driven Doug Leone — succession before falling asleep at wheel. Contrasts Kleiner Perkins' entrepreneur-first style. Don's Stanford talk: invest in markets, augment/replace management, questions ≤20 words.
important_facts: Ben cites Sequoia-backed companies at ~$3.3 trillion aggregate public market capitalization (part 1 scope) — versus ~$1T cited for NeXT acquisition episode as comparison.

Fairchild under Don grew from ~$2M to ~$150M+ annual sales in a few years (late 1950s–60s dollars); Don ran National Semiconductor earnings calls when CEO Charlie Sporck refused — meeting Capital Group LPs.
1972 Capital Group "Sequoia Fund" = $5M; independent Sequoia Capital Fund I ≈ $3–5M raised over ~3 years (1974) — entire proto-VC industry met monthly at SF Mark Hopkins hotel.
Sequoia invested ~$600K initial in Atari (already ~$30M revenue); ~$2M total in Apple early — distributed ~$6M Apple gains within ~2 years for LP tax bills, missing multi-decade compounding.
Cisco (1987): $2.5M for ~30% from Stanford campus router founders; IPO ~$160M+; Don stayed chairman into mid-1990s — "We network networks" as ideal pitch.

mental_model.name: Market First, Active Partner
mental_model.components: Don inverted engineer-founder VC: pick massive markets about to explode from Fairchild/National roadmaps, back teams receptive to help, accept replacing founders if needed ("Genghis Khan" quote). Socratic 20-word questions test storytelling clarity — proxy for focus/speed/stealth. Early funds concentrated capital (often only private round) into hardware with manufacturing — high check sizes vs today's seed. Lesson from Apple: distribution for tax liquidity destroys compounders; Cisco proved holding public winners still wins.
mental_model.application: Seed investors should separate market-size conviction from founder polish — but require coachability. Fund size and hold period must match winner magnitude; early exits on generational companies dominate IRR errors. Succession planning (Moritz, Leone) preserves firm brand beyond founder.

competitive_advantage: Sequoia's moat in this era: Don's Fairchild/National pipeline (sourcing), technical roadmap foresight (diligence), Capital Group credibility (fundraising), and willingness to be toughest board member (governance). Raiding Xerox PARC/IBM for commercializable tech after Apple success. Weaknesses: software blind spot (Oracle bootstrapped $2K, anti-VC Larry Ellison); Apple sale; hardware-margin mindset early. Kleiner competed with founder-centric model — both worked. Enduring ethos: "partnership" language, long-term public holds (post-Apple trauma), multi-generation partner handoffs — part 1 closes teasing Doug Leone and Mike Moritz's generation scaling Sequoia into today's $12+ billion global growth platform. Trip Hawkins/EA chose Don for forced scale. Oracle anti-VC rhetoric then Sequoia partnership at year six. Notre Dame endowment best manager over 30 years supports A+ grading debate. Wave Capital founders cite Greg McAdoo alignment lesson from Sequoia history.

key_insights.view: Don invented VC by treating startups as sales territories.
key_insights.question: Why did Fairchild prepare Don for Sequoia?
key_insights.answer: He sold novel tech to defense then diversified applications despite parent bureaucracy — same as picking startup markets. Personal angel checks when board said no proved model; Capital Group scaled checks from thousands to millions.

key_insights.view: Apple distribution is Sequoia's costliest lesson.
key_insights.question: What went wrong with Apple?
key_insights.answer: Fund I had taxable corporate LPs needing 1979 distributions; Don sold Apple stake for ~$6M total fund gain. Apple became trillion-dollar company; Sequoia learned to hold winners public for decades (Airbnb/Google cited).

key_insights.view: Cisco refined the playbook Apple broke.
key_insights.question: Why is Cisco the template?
key_insights.answer: $2.5M upfront for 30%, fast IPO, Don stayed chairman, no early dump. Sandy/Len non-traditional founders OK because router market exploding. Three-word pitch discipline.

key_insights.view: Markets over founders — but founders must listen.
key_insights.question: How reconcile Don's founder assessment with "invest in markets"?
key_insights.answer: Market size is necessary; team must execute with focus and accept Sequoia operating help. Engineers alone (traitorous eight style) lacked go-to-market; Don added sales/marketing/finance DNA — sometimes replacing CEOs.

key_insights.view: Would Silicon Valley exist without Don?
key_insights.question: What would have happened otherwise?
key_insights.answer: Ben leans Don accelerated ecosystem; David notes Kleiner Perkins parallel success — capital finds opportunities. Don's specific contribution: active company-building plus semiconductor ecosystem map at inflection moment.

top_investment_implications.thesis: Modern semiconductor consolidation descends from Fairchild diaspora Don sold; AVGO/Broadcom lineage reflects M&A roll-up of chip franchises Sequoia first financed.

golden_quotes: Give me a giant market always. — Don Valentine on preferring huge markets even with imperfect CEOs.
Questions can only be 20 words or less or I'll kill you. — Don at Stanford on Socratic VC method.
We network networks. — Cisco's three-word pitch Sequoia still cites as ideal clarity.

events.event: Traitorous eight leave Shockley; Fairchild Semiconductor founded
Don joins Fairchild; scales sales to $150M+; personal angel investing
Intel founded; Don moves to National Semi; meets Capital Group via earnings calls
Capital Group launches $5M Sequoia Fund; Don full-time startup investing
Independent Sequoia Capital Fund I (~$3–5M) closes after 3-year raise
Atari ~$600K; Apple investment via Markkula; Mike Scott president
Sequoia distributes Apple gains (~$6M) for LP taxes — costly lesson
Cisco $2.5M for 30%; IPO; Don long-term board chair
Mike Moritz and Doug Leone join; succession begins
